title: Cat Eyelashes Rubbing Against Eyeball: Entropion Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about cat eyelashes rubbing against the eye, why it is a medium-urgency concern, and how photos help your vet provide the best care.
content_html:
Understanding Inward-Turning Eyelashes

When you notice your cat's eyelashes or eyelid fur rubbing against their eyeball, it is typically a condition known as Entropion. This occurs when the eyelid rolls inward, causing the hair to scrape against the sensitive cornea. This constant friction is not only painful but can lead to serious complications such as corneal ulcers or permanent scarring.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for this condition is Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a painful condition that requires prompt veterinary attention. You should aim to have your cat seen by a veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. If you notice signs of severe distress, such as the eye being held shut, thick yellow discharge, or a change in the color of the eyeball itself, contact an urgent care clinic immediately.

Why a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your cat's eye can be incredibly beneficial for your veterinary team. Because cats often squint or hide their discomfort when stressed at a clinic, a photo taken in a relaxed environment allows the vet to see the natural position of the eyelid. This visual evidence helps the medical team determine the severity of the irritation and prioritize your cat's appointment based on the risk of corneal damage.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops to soothe my cat's eye?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human eye medications on a cat without a veterinarian's approval, as some ingredients can be toxic or worsen an undiagnosed corneal ulcer.

faqs.question: Will my cat need surgery for this?
faqs.answer: In many cases, surgical correction is the only permanent solution for Entropion to prevent the eyelid from rolling inward and causing chronic pain.

faqs.question: What are the common signs of eye irritation in cats?
faqs.answer: Common signs include squinting, excessive tearing (epiphora), pawing at the face, redness in the whites of the eyes, and cloudiness on the surface of the eye.
